title: Urinary Incontinence in the Older Adult: Types and Behavioral Management
subtitle: A 78-year-old woman reports leaking urine and frequent urgency, with mild perineal skin redness. The nurse identifies the type of incontinence, starts first-line behavioral therapy, and protects skin and dignity — without restricting fluids or rushing to a catheter.

patient.name: Marjorie Flynn
patient.admitDate: Rehab unit. Reports urgency with leakage before reaching the toilet plus occasional leaking with coughing; uses a walker, the bathroom is far, and she has nocturia. Mild perineal redness from moisture. Here for continence assessment and management.

vitals.temp: Uses walker; bathroom far; nocturia x3

nursesNote: Marjorie Flynn, 78, with mixed symptoms — urge leakage plus occasional stress leakage — compounded by limited mobility (walker, distant bathroom) and mild perineal redness. Incontinence is common but NOT normal aging and is treatable. Plan: screen for reversible causes (UTI, meds, constipation), identify the type with a bladder diary and post-void residual, and start first-line behavioral therapy (timed/prompted voiding, pelvic floor exercises, bladder training). Optimize fluids (adequate intake, limit caffeine/evening fluids — do not restrict), protect the skin, ensure easy toilet access and dignity, and avoid an indwelling catheter except as a last resort.
providerOrders: Assess incontinence pattern; bladder diary and post-void residual
Screen for and treat reversible causes (UTI, constipation, medications)
Begin behavioral therapy: timed/prompted voiding, pelvic floor exercises, bladder training
Optimize fluids (adequate intake; limit caffeine/alcohol/evening fluids); protect skin
Avoid indwelling catheter except as a last resort

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What should the nurse know about incontinence?

opts.text: Urinary incontinence is common in older adults but is NOT a normal part of aging
It should be evaluated for reversible causes (UTI, medications, constipation)
Types include stress, urge, overflow, and functional incontinence
Behavioral strategies are first-line treatment
Incontinence is untreatable and should just be managed with pads
It raises the risk of skin breakdown, falls, and social isolation
Stress incontinence leaks with coughing/exertion; urge involves sudden urgency
A bladder diary and post-void residual help identify the type
Pelvic floor (Kegel) exercises help stress and urge incontinence
Scheduled/prompted voiding and bladder training are effective
Restricting fluids is the best way to reduce incontinence
An indwelling catheter should be avoided except as a last resort
Assess the pattern and screen for reversible causes (UTI, meds, constipation)
Identify the type of incontinence
Start behavioral measures (timed voiding, pelvic floor, fluid management)
Protect the skin and provide easy toilet access and dignity
Reassess response; consider medication/referral; document

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Which type of incontinence does each describe?

opts.text: Leaks urine when coughing, sneezing, or lifting
Leakage with laughing or exercise
Sudden strong urge with leakage before reaching the toilet
Frequent urgency and nocturia from an overactive bladder
Constant dribbling with a sense of incomplete emptying (high residual)
Retention from an enlarged prostate causing dribbling leakage
Normal bladder, but can't reach the bathroom in time due to mobility
Reaches the toilet too late because of a walker and a far bathroom

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Review the bladder diary and rule out a UTI or other reversible cause
Teach pelvic floor exercises and a scheduled-voiding plan
Optimize fluids (adequate intake; limit caffeine/evening fluids)
Provide skin protection and accessible toileting; treat constipation
Evaluate progress and refer or add therapy as needed; document
Scheduled voiding and pelvic floor exercises reduce leakage episodes
Skin stays intact with barrier cream and prompt cleansing
Adequate hydration with less caffeine improves urgency
She reaches the commode in time with easy-access clothing
Fluids are restricted, urine concentrates, and urgency worsens
An indwelling catheter is placed for convenience and a CAUTI develops
Untreated incontinence leads to perineal skin breakdown
Incontinence is dismissed as "normal aging" and never evaluated
